challenge.title: Korak 20
challenge.description:
Unutar vašeg elementa od ul, kreirajte šest elemenata od li. Dodajte element h4 sa class postavljenim na list-subtitle i element od p za svaki vaš element od li.

Zatim izvučite sledeći tekst za elemente h4 i p, po redu, gde će svaki h4 koristiti ono što je sa leve strane vertikalnog separatora (pipe), a svaki p će koristiti ono što je sa desne strane:

  • V1 - 2014: We launched freeCodeCamp with a simple list of 15 resources, including Harvard's CS50 and Stanford's Database Class.
  • V2 - 2015: We added interactive algorithm challenges.
  • V3 - 2015: We added our own HTML+CSS challenges (before we'd been relying on General Assembly's Dash course for these).
  • V4 - 2016: We expanded the curriculum to 3 certifications, including Front-End, Back-End, and Data Visualization. They each had 10 required projects, but only the Front-End section had its own challenges. For the other certs, we were still using external resources like Node School.
  • V5 - 2017: We added the back-end and data visualization challenges.
  • V6 - 2018: We launched 6 new certifications to replace our old ones. This was the biggest curriculum improvement to date.

challengeFiles.name: index
challengeFiles.contents:
OUR NEW CURRICULUM

Our efforts to restructure our curriculum with a more project-based focus

By freeCodeCamp

March 7, 2019

Soon the freeCodeCamp curriculum will be 100% project-driven learning. Instead of a series of coding challenges, you'll learn through building projects - step by step. Before we get into the details, let me emphasize: we are not changing the certifications. All 6 certifications will still have the same 5 required projects. We are only changing the optional coding challenges.

After years - years - of pondering these two problems and how to solve them, I slipped, hit my head on the sink, and when I came to I had a revelation! A vision! A picture in my head! A picture of this! This is what makes time travel possible: the flux capacitor!

It wasn't as dramatic as Doc's revelation in Back to the Future. It just occurred to me while I was going for a run. The revelation: the entire curriculum should be a series of projects. Instead of individual coding challenges, we'll just have projects, each with their own seamless series of tests. Each test gives you just enough information to figure out how to get it to pass. (And you can view hints if that isn't enough.)

The entire curriculum should be a series of projects

No more walls of explanatory text. No more walls of tests. Just one test at a time, as you build up a working project. Over the course of passing thousands of tests, you build up projects and your own understanding of coding fundamentals. There is no transition between lessons and projects, because the lessons themselves are baked into projects. And there's plenty of repetition to help you retain everything because - hey - building projects in real life has plenty of repetition.

The main design challenge is taking what is currently paragraphs of explanation and instructions and packing them into a single test description text. Each project will involve dozens of tests like this. People will be coding the entire time, rather than switching back and forth from "reading mode" to "coding mode".

Instead of a series of coding challenges, people will be in their code editor passing one test after another, quickly building up a project. People will get into a real flow state, similar to what they experience when they build the required projects at the end of each certification. They'll get that sense of forward progress right from the beginning. And freeCodeCamp will be a much smoother experience.

A Brief History

Of the Curriculum

tests.text: Vaša komponenta od ul treba da ima šest komponenti od li.
Svaki novi element li mora imati element h4 i p.
tests.testString:
const lis = [...document.querySelectorAll('.lists li')];
assert(lis?.every(li => li?.children?.[0]?.localName === 'h4' && li?.children?.[1]?.localName === 'p'));

tests.text: Tvoj prvi element h4 treba da sadrži tekst V1 - 2014.
Tvoj prvi element p treba da sadrži tekst We launched freeCodeCamp with a simple list of 15 resources, including Harvard's CS50 and Stanford's Database Class.
tests.testString: assert(document.querySelectorAll('.lists li p')?.[0]?.innerText === 'We launched freeCodeCamp with a simple list of 15 resources, including Harvard\'s CS50 and Stanford\'s Database Class.');

tests.text: Tvoj drugi element h4 treba da sadrži tekst V2 - 2015.
p Tvoj drugi deo treba da ima tekst od We added interactive algorithm challenges.
tests.testString: assert(document.querySelectorAll('.lists li p')?.[1]?.innerText === 'We added interactive algorithm challenges.');

tests.text: h4 tvoj treći treba da ima tekst od V3 - 2015.
p tvoj treći treba da ima tekst od We added our own HTML+CSS challenges (before we'd been relying on General Assembly's Dash course for these).
tests.testString: assert(document.querySelectorAll('.lists li p')?.[2]?.innerText === 'We added our own HTML+CSS challenges (before we\'d been relying on General Assembly\'s Dash course for these).');

tests.text: h4 četvrti deo bi trebalo da ima tekst od V4 - 2016.

p Tvoja četvrta bi trebalo da ima tekst od We expanded the curriculum to 3 certifications, including Front-End, Back-End, and Data Visualization. They each had 10 required projects, but only the Front-End section had its own challenges. For the other certs, we were still using external resources like Node School.
tests.testString: assert(document.querySelectorAll('.lists li p')?.[3]?.innerText === 'We expanded the curriculum to 3 certifications, including Front-End, Back-End, and Data Visualization. They each had 10 required projects, but only the Front-End section had its own challenges. For the other certs, we were still using external resources like Node School.');

tests.text: h4 Tvoja peta treba da ima tekst od V5 - 2017.
p Vaš peti treba da ima tekst od We added the back-end and data visualization challenges.
tests.testString: assert(document.querySelectorAll('.lists li p')?.[4]?.innerText === 'We added the back-end and data visualization challenges.');

tests.text: h4 Vaš šesti treba da ima tekst sa V6 - 2018.
Tvoj šesti element p treba da sadrži tekst We launched 6 new certifications to replace our old ones. This was the biggest curriculum improvement to date.
tests.testString: assert(document.querySelectorAll('.lists li p')?.[5]?.innerText === 'We launched 6 new certifications to replace our old ones. This was the biggest curriculum improvement to date.');

tests.text: Šest vaših elemenata sa h4 treba da se odvuče iz klase objekata list-subtitle.

OUR NEW CURRICULUM

Our efforts to restructure our curriculum with a more project-based focus

By freeCodeCamp

March 7, 2019

Soon the freeCodeCamp curriculum will be 100% project-driven learning. Instead of a series of coding challenges, you'll learn through building projects - step by step. Before we get into the details, let me emphasize: we are not changing the certifications. All 6 certifications will still have the same 5 required projects. We are only changing the optional coding challenges.

After years - years - of pondering these two problems and how to solve them, I slipped, hit my head on the sink, and when I came to I had a revelation! A vision! A picture in my head! A picture of this! This is what makes time travel possible: the flux capacitor!

It wasn't as dramatic as Doc's revelation in Back to the Future. It just occurred to me while I was going for a run. The revelation: the entire curriculum should be a series of projects. Instead of individual coding challenges, we'll just have projects, each with their own seamless series of tests. Each test gives you just enough information to figure out how to get it to pass. (And you can view hints if that isn't enough.)

The entire curriculum should be a series of projects

No more walls of explanatory text. No more walls of tests. Just one test at a time, as you build up a working project. Over the course of passing thousands of tests, you build up projects and your own understanding of coding fundamentals. There is no transition between lessons and projects, because the lessons themselves are baked into projects. And there's plenty of repetition to help you retain everything because - hey - building projects in real life has plenty of repetition.

The main design challenge is taking what is currently paragraphs of explanation and instructions and packing them into a single test description text. Each project will involve dozens of tests like this. People will be coding the entire time, rather than switching back and forth from "reading mode" to "coding mode".

Instead of a series of coding challenges, people will be in their code editor passing one test after another, quickly building up a project. People will get into a real flow state, similar to what they experience when they build the required projects at the end of each certification. They'll get that sense of forward progress right from the beginning. And freeCodeCamp will be a much smoother experience.

A Brief History

Of the Curriculum

  • V1 - 2014

    We launched freeCodeCamp with a simple list of 15 resources, including Harvard's CS50 and Stanford's Database Class.

  • V2 - 2015

    We added interactive algorithm challenges.

  • V3 - 2015

    We added our own HTML+CSS challenges (before we'd been relying on General Assembly's Dash course for these).

  • V4 - 2016

    We expanded the curriculum to 3 certifications, including Front-End, Back-End, and Data Visualization. They each had 10 required projects, but only the Front-End section had its own challenges. For the other certs, we were still using external resources like Node School.

  • V5 - 2017

    We added the back-end and data visualization challenges.

  • V6 - 2018

    We launched 6 new certifications to replace our old ones. This was the biggest curriculum improvement to date.

The millions of people who are learning to code through freeCodeCamp will have an even better resource to help them learn these fundamentals.
